commentBody: Yikes, that was hard to get all right, bit all's well that ends well.
Too difficult
Definitely tough, but fun. This is the longest I've taken on a Sunday puzzle in over 5 years. I feel like I earned a cookie.
Nice puzzle. I think quite aligned with Wednesday difficulty.
Too difficult. Did not enjoy it.
Not my cup of tea, but a challenging solve nevertheless.

Oh wow, what a trip! That's what I call a Sunday puzzle. You totally need to understand the theme in order to figure out the roundabout entries because of the “unchecked” squares, which of course are checked by the trick.  

I caught on with MICHELLE, MICHELOB and MICHELIN MAN. It was off to the races from there. 

Thanks, Michael and Oliver. That was so much fun! You get my vote for Sunday POY.
Pretty fun. UTMOST instead of ATMOST cost me probably ten minutes lol. I guess I need to learn my Indian flours.
Saw the roundabouts, but had to remind myself that I had to turn right on entry instead of my usual left

The constructors write:
     "We were told that the test solvers found the gimmick clear enough without the extra help, and we hope that this was your experience as well."

Schlossbergs,
It was my experience too. We'll see how many solvers agree. Thanks for the enjoyable spin(s) around town.

P.S. I hope those who had a hard time figuring out the themers will still find some SILVERLININGS.

When I drew a perfect circle, I got a round of applause.

(I would try it in 3D, but I sphere the worst.)

I slogged through this like a fly flitting inside a mayonnaise jar. I was total rudderless. Not as bad as getting a root canal, but not too far behind. For those who loved this puzzle, congrats. I wish I could have joined you.

Greetings all! I’m repeating the plea to have the navigation changed so that one can, once again, toggle between Wordplay and the puzzle on a phone. It’s incredibly inconvenient to have it all in the same window. 
Please recommend, fellow solvers!

So…I don’t like to brag.

But.

I just had a cosmo here in Paris
where I’m ON VACAY.

Ergo-
(I think you’re picking up what I’m putting down)

the puzzles are about…*me.*

I didn’t see that coming either,
but here we are.

In a roundabout way.



But, guys.
I want you to treat me like I am an average person.

(And I’m not even gonna *mention* that I saw a 
picture of Michelle Obama today.
It’s beyond eerie.)


[ *shiver* ]

Anyone ever had the fun experience of doing a roundabout like this:
MARTINITINITINI GLASS?

Maybe while your passengers are shouting instructions?

Maybe one of those that has an inner circle and an outer circle?

Gimmick is the word. This puzzle is way too reliant on that. When the theme stops being clever and starts doing all the work, it stops being fun. You’re not solving—you’re just decoding a trick over and over again. A good crossword feels like a conversation with the constructor; this one feels like being hit with the same joke on repeat.
So that I can move on from this puzzle, I'm gonna have some Armagnac in a martini glass.  Maybe a couple more.
Difficult but extremely clever. Loved the challenge.
Were the four letters in each traffic circle supposed to spell something?  Or were there really 16 unchecked squares in this puzzle?  Somebody tell me I'm missing something.

Excellent puzzle.  Was not obvious to me how the roundabouts worked; a ton of fun figuring it out!

Only one quibble.  As far as I can tell, the letter at the top of the roundabout was only covered by a single clue.   (In contrast, the other letters in the roundabout had to work for two or more clue/answer pairs.)  So  if one did not know the spelling of the word that went north from the roundabout, no help was available from another clue/answer pair.  This seems unusual for a nytimes format puzzle.
Never been happier to go way over my average time. This puzzle was a masterful creation, and a pleasure to solve.

Great challenging puzzle. I tried multiple methods of coping with the roundabouts, including a rebus BUTT at one point. Things finally clicked with ARMAGEDDON when I couldn’t make sense of NODD_. That gave me ARMAGNAC and finally ARMANIS. 

A tough solve but not the end of the world.

Well, I very much dislike roundabouts! Nevertheless, I enjoyed the puzzle. I was a little surprised that you entered all of them the same place, from the left. I thought they it might rotate where you drove in. Anyhow, fun puzzle. Maybe this puzzle will help me be less grumpy when I encounter a roundabout. They are increasing in these here parts. 

I came in about average on this. Lost some time in the far upper right corner. TEEPEES and BUTTONFLIES was all I had there for a while. Looking back, other than ASGARD and APOLO, I'm not sure why it seemed tougher to me. 

Got a good chuckle out of 122A for UCLA! 

Lots of folks made some good trouble today—and this was good fun!
Loved it, learned a lot, like translation of DALAI LAMA.  Google n-grams tells me... FOGEY only became more common than FOGY in about 1980... FOGY more common before that.
I don't have anything to add that hasn't already been said, lol, I also just really enjoyed this puzzle
Ugh, as much as I love Indian food I had UTTA for ATTA at the top left, which still worked for the across as UTMOST.  This one square failed the puzzle for me.
Loved this puzzle
This isn’t the kind of puzzle that I enjoy solving. Enough said.
I had to reverse engineer the fill in the top left roundabout. Now I think I get how it works. I will absolutely forget for next time. Fun though.
A roundabout leads one to a turn-off.  Ironically, for me, this puzzle did quite the contrary.

So I'm not a fan of roundabouts. So much so, in fact, that  I decided to take a Lyft to today's NO KINGS rally in town rather than cope with a particularly daunting cluster between my house and my destination.

Things were going swimmingly and then --  cruuunch!  My poor driver got sideswiped by another car's ill-timed lane change. 

No one was hurt.  Both cars sustained a bit of damage. It just took several tense minutes to figure it all out. And I did arrive safely at the rally, although a bit later than planned.

I applaud the verisimilitude of this puzzle. It recreated the roundabout experience with amazing accuracy. 

(Seriously, though, very clever puzzle.  I'm just an old fogy with a terrible sense of direction.)

Not happy that the format of the roundabout entries was just the next, single letter. I wasted a lot of time putting BUTT, BUTTO, BUTTON as rebuses, etc., then trying to figure out what answer I might have missed elsewhere, then having to go back to everything after the puzzle wasn't marked complete, and changing it to T, O, N, etc.

Probably wasted about 10 minutes on all that nonsense, and got way over my Sunday average.
I struggled with the fill a bit and then took a while to catch on to the way the theme worked. A longer Sunday solve than usual for me but I enjoyed it.
My brain is upside down - or possibly nwod edispu. Thanks for the egnellahc.

Too easy? Too hard? Who cares?

This was really fun.
I was really hoping that "ocean monk" would be PERE DU MER, but Dalai Lama is also acceptable.

This was like a combination Sunday/Thursday puzzle. I could not have done it without reading the wordplay to figure out what the heck was going on. That always makes solving seem a little special. Killer entries too like asgard, apolo, cronus and I still don't know what gray and yellow age is/are. So not a favorite but I appreciate the effort that went into it.
I wish we were able to give some sort of “favorite point” to puzzles we like best, because I definitely would give one to this puzzle!

I loved figuring it out without extra help. That's the whole fun of it! Very clever and solvable. 81A was a bit of a stretch, clue-wise, but others like 54A and 122A made up for it. Great job, Schlossbergs!
That was a piece of work!
Major kudos to the constructor! This was an amazing puzzle, and I really enjoyed seeing the theme play out around the roundabouts.
SO creative!  SO fun!  I don't know how they managed this one but I was really impressed by all the circuitous craftings.  BRAVO!

As a Brit I have to admit I've been chuckling at all the fear and confusion that roundabouts apparently evoke in our American cousins! Roundabouts to me are just normal and harmless and useful!
Took me a long while to get the theme idea, mainly because since the whole point of roundabouts is that you can enter / exit anywhere so I didn't think to always start on the left. Good one once it clicked, though.
Nice - but challenging - Sunday ... thanks!
Quite enjoyed this puzzle! It took me awhile to figure out the precise mechanic. I could tell I was on to something and I enjoyed figuring it out. Have to admit, I have never heard of ARMAGNAC.

Ok, this is frustrating.  I’m at a 99 day streak (my longest ever) and needing the gold star for this puzzle to reach 100!  To my eye, I think I have this puzzle solved.  But I’m not getting the oh so coveted (particularly in this case) yellow star.  Is there something special I need to do with the “roundabouts” that I am missing?  I get that the across entry leading into each roundabout from the West combines with the three exits (South, East and North) of the roundabout to form answers to those respective clues (recognizing that the South, East, and North first letters are additive… i.e. the East solution includes the first letter of the South entry; and the North solution includes the first letter of both the South and East entries), and the answer to the North entry runs from bottom to top.  For example, the answer to 72D is MICHELINMAN.  Is there anything else I need to do?  PLEASE, NO HINTS OR CLUES to the solutions as I am very strict with myself about no lookups or cheats.

If it’s not something odd about how to input the roundabouts, then I’ll have to flyspeck yet again, but I’ve literally revisited every entry of the puzzle three times, with progressively more attention each time, and I simply do not see an error (admittedly this has happened a couple of times in the past and, well, there has been an error!)  Thank you in advance!
that's the hardest sunday i think i ever did.  good, but hard.  kind of a thursday sunday.
Nice puzzle! Getting the correct spelling while going around the roundabouts was a challenge, but I got there, phew! Now it's time for a martini.
Thank you for the Yes Roundabout link. Saved me the trouble of posting one. :-)

What a great puzzle!  Maybe my favorite to date???  It was challenging the entire way through without feeling un-fun or sloggy, and the moment I finally got the entirety of the gimmick I was quite charmed.

Well done for making me enjoy a Sunday xword!
I was looking for COGNAC because I am not familiar with ARMAGNAC. That's why it took me 2:48.  That one thing! What a puzzle!
A good challenging Saturday night Sunday puzzle

Loved every moment of that.
Tedious

Then COPSE and SEEST, obscure vocabulary words sharing awkward crossing words like RIFEST and ONE (A plain division answer looking like it's about eyesight).

Other than those little frustrating regions though, great puzzle.
LOVED IT!!

Had to work a bit harder to see how the other exits worked, keeping the first word to build, but finally saw glass or “ssalg” and was able to get martini going around. commentBody:
For me the usual compliment and complaint together of late.

Compliplaint?

The constructing?  Clever as all get out.  10/10.

The cluing?  Frequently Tuesday level.

Fun though.
Absolutely brilliant.  I can't imagine thinking this up, and implementing it must've been a major challenge.  Just great.  Many thanks.

Nice concept. 
Took me longer than I would have liked to figure out how to use the roundabout, but eventually got there. 

But that NE corner just killed me. I couldn’t get a confident toehold. Figured it was TEEPEE, but leanto worked with Anton (APOLlO Anton Ohno). I knew I knew the Norse world, I could pull it. And I thought the gun was a hAUSER. 

Had to wave the white flag and do a check puzzle. 
But honestly, I prefer that to doing an easy boring puzzle.

This was a clever puzzle, but a bit complicated to enter.  With my big thumbs, it's easy to make typos.  With such a complicated grid to fill in,  it was hard to find the typos, so I had to check the puzzle to figure it out. Oh well.

The Family Schlossberg with a wonderful puzzle. 

Very fun! Congratulations, guys!!

Once I got this theme I figured it would be pretty polarizing and so it was, based on the comments so far. I was a little confused and off put by the different directions at first but once the trick became clear it was very consistent and logical. This is definitely a constructor’s puzzle—complex and structurally intricate, especially considering the constraints of three words starting with the same beginning but different roots (I certainly wouldn’t fault MICHELIN and MICHELLE). Throw in the  symmetry requirements (notice the clever mirror symmetry)  and all the additional fill, which was very solid considering the difficult theme, and you have a remarkable achievement. Beyond that as I solver I had an enjoyable challenge and a fun time working out the theme. This will be on my POY short list, I think.
One head scratcher as someone familiar with Hebrew—couldn’t figure out how anyone gets SUE from Shoshana, which means Rose in Hebrew, and certainly nobody actually named Shoshana would have Sue as a nickname. But with further research it appears Susannah (and by extension Susan) all come from that root, so that was actually a very interesting way to clue it.
Now that's a New York Times Sunday crossword puzzle!  Well done!
Interesting puzzle. I didn’t like ‘seized with the teeth’ as ‘bit off’ though….it doesn’t imply or mean that at all; to me that’s more like ‘gripping’ … anyhoo, other than that, quite clever.

An absolute delight!  I both wanted to finish it right away and savor the experience at the same time.  Bravo!

Once I got it, I enjoyed practicing my backward spelling on the entries that exited the top of the roundabouts.  A lovely start to my Sunday.
Very cool puzzle—loved the clues and the roundabouts were great! Nicely done.

My favorite Sunday since Lewis Rothlein and Jeff Chen’s “Name Dropping” (4/30/23).
A fascinating fun fact of no importance whatsoever--Carmel, Indiana, a suburb of Indianapolis, population 104,000. has more round-abouts than any other city in the United States. I hate round-abouts.

I love the backstory, that this was crafted by a father and his teenage son.  And it’s not a case of the son throwing out a suggestion here and there and basically being credited as a NYT constructor as a gift from Dad.  No, Oliver’s role – writer of the program that generated the theme answers, played an integral role.  

Just an incredible grid build, from having the theme clusters be symmetrical, to having five (!) of them, to having the four answers of each roundabout come from different etymologies.  Wowwowwow!

But that’s not all.  There were seven lovely NYT answer debuts, including four that begged the question, “How could these never been in the Times puzzle before?”: TRAILHEAD, MARTINI GLASS, MICHELOB, and MICHELIN MAN.

Plus, the funnest word I’ve run into in a long time – LAMINANO (25D)!  Yes, it’s meant to be read from south to north, but forget that.  It’s a new word, it’s fun to say, and it means “a thin layer”.  LAMINANO!

Bravo, Michael and Oliver.  Bravo squared.  This was for me a fun and satisfying tour de force.  More please, and thank you!
OMG. What fun. So clever and challenging. Figuring out the roundabout trick was very rewarding!!!

While echoing others here who have commended this crossword and its clever construction, I found it frustrating. Ignorance on my part.

Same with the mascot, the brand, the mayor, the Orioles, the suits - not knowing them really distracted me from seeing how this worked. I didn't know the proper names either, (Orbitz, Apolo, ALMA, DMZ) but that's usual for me.

I could see GLASS was upside down, but that didn't really help! 

I think of a FOGEY as someone strait-laced and conservative, while a 'geezer' is smart and streetwise! I thought DO I GOTTA couldn't possibly be right. But I got SMORE this time.

Some very busy roundabouts here also have traffic lights, so one goes around stop-start, but at least you get around. In the Midlands, UK they are called 'traffic islands' (or 'oi-lands')

Found this one more challenging than a typical Sunday, even though I understood the theme quite early. The odd (to me) spelling CRONUS tripped me up for a bit, and sevaral of the thematic entries required some thinking as well: MARTINET, MICHELINMAN, ARMAGNAC.

I suppose the editorial team felt it advisable to include the signage to point out that these "roundabouts" should be traversed in the American rather than British way.

Great puzzle, my applause and thanks alike go to the constructors and editors 👏

This is one of those Sundays where not only was the gimmick itself tough to fully deduce (or at least it was for me), but there was some crunchy/obscure fill to go along with it, eg EDERLE.

The net result: toughest Sunday in recent memory, and a very fun solve.
Myeah, too much for me. I see those little diagrams as stop signs. Got better things to do.

I prefer to solve puzzles with overlays off. That way (when there is some thematic overlay) I get to solve an additional puzzle 🤓. Two puzzles for the price of one 😊. I also do not like read the Sunday puzzle title in advance (no hints). I’m glad that the title is conveniently hidden in the app.

Still, I managed to figure out the theme and solve the puzzle about 25% faster than usual. The cluing (particularly of the themers) seemed fairly easy (or just luckily in my wheelhouse). 

However, I’m always a little upset when the final grid (taken by itself) includes gobbledygook entries (SSALGI, etc). I strongly prefer the filled content (read in the traditional directions) to ALSO be valid fill, and when they are not, I am left with this question: “was the cleverness of the theme worth breaking this convention?”

From the comments, I guess that most people would give a resounding “yes” .  I’m on the fence for this one. Given the complexity of the theme, it would likely be impossible to find candidates for a single roundabout that would work both ways, let alone for five of them, so I get it. 

Kudos on the construction though.

Today I Learned that DALAI LAMA means "Ocean monk".

I knew that LAMA meant "monk" or "teacher" and had assumed that DALAI meant highest or greatest.

PS I would not say that roundabouts are "similar to" rotaries and traffic circles. Roundabouts, rotaries, and traffic circles all mean the same thing. (Rotary is a New England word, roundabout is mainly British, and traffic circle is the generic U.S. term.)

Dan,
In lay terminology you're right, but the technical article in the link in the column distinguishes between roundabouts and rotaries, and calls traffic circles a non-technical term. Waiting for the distinctions to show up in a future crossword.

It may be confusing, but: The words "rotary", "roundabout", and "traffic circle" have varying frequencies of use depending on time and place. Likewise, the styles of circular traffic paths have *also* varied with time and place. Therefore the three terms will most typically apply to different distributions of ways the the circular traffic path is executed.* But the terms mean exactly the same thing.
—————
* For example I once waited almost 10 minutes to cross the street safely from the center of a traffic circle in Chiang Mai around a city block, which showed no concern for pedestrians.
Just as the term "human being" has a varied set of examples depending on time and place. But they're all human beings.

@Mike There were 5 unchecked squares in the puzzle, the north square of each of the roundabouts. The 3 other squares in each roundabout had to work with 4, 3, or 2 answers.

All four squares adjacent to each roundabout are not conventionally checked (i.e., in an Across and a Down answer). The "entry" adjacent square *is* checked by the theme because it must work for four answers; the other three are on their own. That will happen with some themes, and it overrides the traditional check convention.

Let me amend my comment to note that Terry is correct that only the letter in the square at the top of the roundabout is unchecked, since the bottom and right squares are checked by more than one answer just like the entry square. The "exception for theme" rule applies just to the top square.

commentBody:
@Lee

You are missing the trick.

Take the one you cited.

Start with the fill to 101A.  MICH.

This is the entry from the left to the roundabout.

And it is the first 4 letters of the south/90-degree turn, the east (or 180-degree) turn and the north/270-degree turn.

Wordplay explains it well, too.

@Lee Quick answers:
- yes you're missing something
- yes there's a rule and yes it's unwritten. Part of the puzzle is figuring it out
- no you're not dumb, just keep at it and you'll get the hang of it
- take for example the answer 'armagnac'. See how you need to go around the roundabout to spell that out?

@Lee
As was pointed out elsewhere, in order to make a comment I think you had to open the Wordplay column. You clearly didn't read it, or if you did you *really* didn't understand it. You're newer here, and so I'm not surprised the puzzle flummoxed you--it was confounding for a lot of us, at least until the penny dropped.

I don't know what a pre course is, but I do know that there are a lot of conventions that you learn as you go. If you're a Sunday-only solver, I suggest you go to the archives and do a bunch of Mondays--they're the easiest day, and over the week the puzzles get progressively harder. Dig up some of the how-to columns--learn how to enter a rebus, how to spot wordplay, what quotation and question marks mean, etc.  You'll soon learn to love it, or you'll learn it's not for you. Either is fine, but If you want to learn you have a support group here.

BTW when you read the column you'll learn that what you were supposed to do with SEILFN was use the roundabout below it; start at 52A and go 3/4 around the roundabout and continue upwards to get BUTTONFLIES.  Ditto for BALLOONANIMAL. ;)

That being said, I've heard neither of Gertrude EDERLE nor the word MARTINET, so the two entries crossing stumped me.

Still, this was one of the most enjoyable Sunday puzzles I have ever solved.

@Katie Roundabouts exist because they save lives. They are much safer than traffic lights because they naturally compel drivers to slow down whereas traffic lights—yellows especially— encourage drivers to speed up. Collisions happen at roundabouts but they are usually just low speed fender benders, whereas the impacts at lighted intersections cause greater damage, injury and death. Also, they basically manage themselves and don’t require the electrical maintenance that lights do or sustain the same kind of damage in storms. In addition, because they are usually green spaces and sometimes contain plantings and community gardens and such (often maintained by volunteers), they are much more attractive and pleasant than lighted intersections. Finally, they encourage motorists to take turns in an active, cooperative, collaborative way rather than imposing a structure (red light stop, green light go, yellow light go very fast) from “above” and in that way they promote social cohesion. They are absolutely a net social good.
